Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Charcoal object moves toward clicked location. Clicking blue (right) moved charcoal right 4 cols. Gray area expands as charcoal moves. Top charcoal eats pink bar. Goal likely involves moving charcoal to specific targets.
- Goal model: Possibly grow/expand charcoal to fill or collect something. Need more data.
- Plan: Click on left side (black bar) to test if charcoal moves toward click location.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me track the charcoal position changes:
- Initial: boundary around [44-49, 46-49] (cols 46-49)
- After click 3 (blue at 25,62): boundary around [44-49, 50-53] (cols 50-53) - moved RIGHT 4 cols
- After click 4 (black at 28,25): boundary still [44-49, 50-53] (cols 50-53) - DID NOT MOVE!
Wait, the charcoal didn't move at all! The boundary is the same as before. But the top charcoal grew from 4 to 5 pixels, and the pink bar went from 60 to 59.
But looking at the ASCII, the charcoal shape seems to be at cols 50-51 still (cc at cols 50-51, cccc at cols 48-51). Wait, let me re-read:
Row 44: 'GGGGGGGGccYY' - cols 44-55, so 'c' at cols 50-51
Row 46: 'GGGGGGccccYY' - 'c' at cols 48-51
Hmm, but the boundary says [[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], ...]. Let me decode the boundary:
[[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], [48, 52], [47, 51], [47, 50], [46, 50], [46, 51], [45, 52]]
This is a clockwise boundary. The columns range from 50 to 53. But the ASCII shows 'c' at cols 48-51 in row 46. There's a discrepancy.
Wait, the ASCII string 'GGGGGGccccYY' at row 46, cols 44-55:
- col 44: G
- col 45: G
- col 46: G
- col 47: G
- col 48: G
- col 49: G
- col 50: c
- col 51: c
- col 52: c
- col 53: c
- col 54: Y
- col 55: Y
So 'c' is at cols 50-53 in row 46. And in row 44:
'GGGGGGGGccYY' at cols 44-55:
- col 44-51: G
- col 52: c
- col 53: c
- col 54: Y
- col 55: Y
So the charcoal is at cols 50-53 in row 46-47 and cols 52-53 in rows 44-45, 48-49. This matches the boundary.
Now, comparing to the previous state (after click 3):
The boundary was the same: [[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], [48, 52], [47, 51], [47, 50], [46, 50], [46, 51], [45, 52]]
So the charcoal DID NOT move after clicking the black bar on the left. But the top charcoal grew from 4 to 5 pixels.
Wait, but earlier I thought the charcoal moved from cols 46-49 to cols 50-53 after clicking the blue square. Let me re-check.
Initial charcoal boundary: [[44, 48], [44, 49], [49, 49], [49, 48], [48, 48], [47, 47], [47, 46], [46, 46], [46, 47], [45, 48]]
This means the charcoal was at cols 46-49 initially.
After click 3 (blue at 25,62): boundary [[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], [48, 52], [47, 51], [47, 50], [46, 50], [46, 51], [45, 52]]
This means the charcoal moved to cols 50-53. So it moved RIGHT by 4 columns.
After click 4 (black at 28,25): boundary still [[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], [48, 52], [47, 51], [47, 50], [46, 50], [46, 51], [45, 52]]
The charcoal did NOT move.
So clicking on the black bar on the left did NOT move the charcoal. But clicking on the blue square on the right DID move it right.
Hmm, maybe the charcoal only moves when you click on certain types of objects? Or maybe it only moves in one direction?
Let me think about this differently. The charcoal moved right when I clicked on the blue square. The blue square is at the right edge. Maybe the charcoal moves toward the edge when you click on objects near that edge?
Or maybe the game is about collecting objects, and the charcoal "attracts" or "repels" based on what you click?
Let me try clicking on the yellow strip to see what happens. The yellow strip is at rows 28-31, cols 38-39 (or around there based on the ASCII).
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. Looking at the board:
- There's a gray area on the left and a white area on the right
- There are black bars, yellow strips, and blue squares
- The charcoal object moves around
- The top charcoal eats the pink bar
Maybe the goal is to move the charcoal to specific locations to "collect" or "transform" objects? Or maybe the goal is to fill the entire board with charcoal?
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on the charcoal object itself and see what happens.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the clicks:
1. Click 1: (46, 47) - on the charcoal object - created top charcoal (1px)
2. Click 2: (46, 47) - on the charcoal object again - grew top charcoal to 3px
3. Click 3: (25, 62) - on the blue square - moved charcoal right 4 cols, grew top charcoal to 4px
4. Click 4: (28, 25) - on the black bar - didn't move charcoal, grew top charcoal to 5px
So clicking on the charcoal itself creates/grows the top charcoal. Clicking on other objects also grows the top charcoal but may or may not move the main charcoal.
The fact that clicking on the blue square moved the charcoal right but clicking on the black bar didn't move it suggests that only certain objects cause movement.
Let me look at the objects more carefully:
- Blue squares (b, 16px): two of them, at right edge
- Black bars (B, 72px and B, 96px): horizontal bars
- Yellow strips (Y, 8px and Y, 12px): vertical strips
- Charcoal (c, 16px): the moving object
Maybe the charcoal only moves when you click on blue objects? Let me test by clicking on a yellow object.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking charcoal creates/grows top charcoal. Clicking blue square moved main charcoal right 4 cols. Clicking black bar didn't move charcoal but grew top charcoal. Only certain objects cause movement.
Plan: Test clicking on yellow strip to see if it causes movement, and try clicking on the other blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state of all objects to track changes
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find yellow objects
print("Yellow objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Find blue objects
print("\nBlue objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Click on the yellow strip (node with Y, 8px, boundary around [28,38]-[31,39])
# From initial data: Node 5: Y, 8px, boundary=[[28, 38], [28, 39], [31, 39], [31, 38]]
print("\nClicking on yellow strip at (30, 38)")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 38}])

# Check charcoal position
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter clicking yellow:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        print(f"  Charcoal: id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'P':
        print(f"  Pink: {n['pixels']}px")

# Compare to before
print("\nCharcoal moved? Checking...")
# Previous boundary was [[44, 52], [44, 53], [49, 53], [49, 52], [48, 52], [47, 51], [47, 50], [46, 50], [46, 51], [45, 52]]
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
